professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been studying how cities try to attract employers this semester. A number of towns now offer large tax breaks to lure a single big factory or headquarters, sometimes worth tens of millions of dollars. Some economists argue these deals pay off because the jobs and spending ripple through the local economy, while others believe the money would do more good spread across schools and roads that benefit everyone. Should local governments offer these targeted tax breaks to attract individual large companies? Why or why not?

students.name: Marco
students.text: I don't think these deals are worth it. The companies often threaten to leave for a better offer elsewhere, so cities end up in a bidding war that drains public budgets. That money could fund infrastructure that helps every business, not just one favored corporation that may relocate again anyway.

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I see the risk, but a major employer can fundamentally reshape a struggling region. When a factory brings thousands of stable jobs, the resulting income and consumer spending often generate far more tax revenue over time than the initial incentive cost. For depressed areas, that catalyst can be decisive.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: For this week's discussion, let's think about how pharmacies are expanding their role in basic care. A growing number of chains now let pharmacists diagnose minor conditions and prescribe medication for things like strep throat or urinary infections, without a doctor's visit. Some health experts welcome this as a way to ease pressure on overcrowded clinics, while others worry it fragments care and risks missing serious problems. Do you think pharmacists should be allowed to diagnose and prescribe for common minor illnesses?

students.name: Jonah
students.text: I'm in favor of expanding their role. Access to a doctor can mean waiting days or traveling far, especially in rural areas, and that delay can worsen treatable conditions. Pharmacists are highly trained professionals, and handling routine cases frees physicians to focus on genuinely complex patients.

students.name: Lena
students.text: I'm more cautious here. Symptoms that look minor can occasionally signal something serious, and a pharmacist working without a full medical history might overlook those interconnected warning signs. I'd rather preserve the continuity that comes from seeing a doctor who knows the whole patient.

professor.name: Dr. Novak
professor.text: We've been examining how universities assess student work this semester. Several departments are now requiring students to submit handwritten, in-class exams instead of take-home essays, largely in response to AI writing tools. Some instructors argue this is the only reliable way to confirm students actually mastered the material, while others believe it punishes thoughtful writing and rewards memorization under pressure. Should universities return to in-class handwritten exams as the main form of assessment? Why or why not?

students.name: Wendy
students.text: I support the shift back. Take-home work has become nearly impossible to verify, and assessment loses its meaning if instructors can't tell whose thinking they're grading. A timed exam, however imperfect, at least demonstrates that the knowledge genuinely belongs to the student.

students.name: Carlos
students.text: I think this overcorrects. Real intellectual work rarely happens in a rushed hour with no resources, and handwritten exams mostly reward fast recall rather than deep analysis. Schools should redesign assignments to require personal reflection and process, not retreat to a format that measures the wrong skills.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: More and more news outlets have begun placing their reporting behind paywalls, charging readers a monthly fee for full access. Supporters argue this is the only sustainable way to fund serious journalism now that advertising revenue has collapsed, while critics counter that locking up the news deepens inequality, since only those who can pay stay well informed. What's your perspective: are paywalls a justified way to support journalism, or do they do more harm than good?

students.name: Phoebe
students.text: I think paywalls are necessary, even if imperfect. Quality investigative reporting is expensive, and for years readers expected it for free while advertising quietly funded it. Asking people to pay directly restores an honest relationship and gives newsrooms the independence to pursue stories that powerful advertisers might dislike.

students.name: Eric
students.text: I worry about the consequences for an informed public. When reliable journalism costs money, many people simply drift toward free, lower-quality sources or outright misinformation. A democracy depends on shared access to facts, so I'd prefer models like donations or public funding that keep the news open to everyone.
